class_schedule.topic: Immersive Learning: VR, AR, MR, and XR Defined
learning_objectives: Distinguish between virtual reality, augmented reality, mixed reality, and extended reality
Explain the concept of presence and its relationship to learning
Evaluate the current evidence base for immersive learning technologies
readings: Bailenson, J. - Experience on Demand Ch. 1-3
Jensen, L. & Konradsen, F. - A Review of the Use of VR Head-Mounted Displays in Education

lecture_notes.introduction: Immersive technologies offer something no other medium can: the feeling of being there. Presence — the psychological sense of being in a virtual environment — is VR's unique affordance. But presence alone does not guarantee learning.
key_concepts: VR: fully immersive, replaces the real world
AR: overlays digital content on the real world
MR: digital and physical objects coexist and interact
Presence: spatial presence, social presence, self-presence
Embodied cognition: the body's role in learning and memory
lecture_notes.demonstration: First VR experience: try three educational VR applications (virtual field trip, lab simulation, historical recreation) and reflect on presence, engagement, and learning

review.value: The illusion of 'being there' in a virtual environment; achieved through immersion (sensory fidelity) and interaction (ability to act within the environment). Stronger presence correlates with stronger emotional responses and memory formation.
Meta-analyses show VR is effective for spatial knowledge, procedural skills, and empathy development. Effects for conceptual learning are mixed and depend heavily on instructional design quality.

class_schedule.topic: When VR Works and When It Does Not
learning_objectives: Identify learning contexts where VR provides unique value over other media
Apply the 'dangerous, impossible, counterproductive, or rare' framework
Evaluate cost-benefit trade-offs of VR implementation
readings: Makransky, G. & Petersen, G.B. - The Cognitive Affective Model of Immersive Learning
Clark, R. - Media Will Never Influence Learning (classic debate piece)

lecture_notes.introduction: VR is expensive, complex, and requires significant design effort. It should only be used when its unique affordances — immersion, presence, embodied interaction — add genuine instructional value that cheaper media cannot provide.
key_concepts: DICER framework: Dangerous, Impossible, Counterproductive, Expensive, or Rare situations
VR for hazardous training: surgery, firefighting, military, chemical spills
VR for impossible experiences: molecular exploration, historical events, space
VR for empathy: perspective-taking (disability simulation, refugee experience)
When NOT to use VR: text-based learning, simple procedures, lecture replacement
lecture_notes.demonstration: DICER analysis: evaluate five proposed VR educational applications and determine which genuinely need VR and which could be served by cheaper alternatives
setup_requirements: Cost comparison data for VR vs. alternatives

review.value: VR is justified when the real experience is Dangerous (surgical training), Impossible (planetary exploration), Counterproductive (bias training), Expensive (flight simulation), or Rare (earthquake response)

Clark argued media are 'mere vehicles' for instruction; Kozma countered that media capabilities interact with instructional methods. VR adds to this debate because its affordances (presence, embodiment) may constitute a genuinely unique instructional capability.

learning_objectives: Apply instructional design principles to VR experience creation
Design for comfort: minimize cybersickness and cognitive overload
Create VR storyboards and interaction flow diagrams
readings: Jerald, J. - The VR Book Ch. 8-10
Makransky, G. et al. - Adding Immersive VR to a Science Lab Simulation

lecture_notes.introduction: Designing for VR is fundamentally different from designing for screens. The learner is inside the experience, not watching it. Every design decision affects comfort, agency, and cognitive load in ways that have no parallel in traditional media.
key_concepts: VR comfort: frame rate, locomotion, visual stability, session duration
Cognitive load in VR: immersion increases both engagement and potential overload
Generative learning in VR: embodied actions that promote deeper processing
Guidance in VR: spatial cues, NPCs, progressive disclosure, gaze guidance
Storyboarding for VR: 360-degree thinking, branching paths, spatial layouts
lecture_notes.demonstration: VR storyboard workshop: create a 5-scene VR educational experience storyboard including spatial layout, interaction points, guidance cues, and learning checkpoints

review.value: Maintain 90+ FPS, avoid unexpected camera movement, use teleportation over smooth locomotion for novice users, limit session duration to 15-20 minutes, provide a stable reference frame

Makransky's CAMIL model: immersion drives presence, which drives interest and motivation, but learning depends on whether the experience triggers generative cognitive processing (enacting, explaining, predicting)

class_schedule.topic: Creating VR Content: 360 Video and Interactive Environments
learning_objectives: Build interactive VR scenes using no-code tools
Compare 360 video, social VR, and interactive VR for different learning contexts
readings: Tutorial: CoSpaces Edu for Educational VR Creation
Comparison of VR creation tools for educators

lecture_notes.introduction: You do not need to be a Unity developer to create educational VR content. This session covers the spectrum of VR creation tools, from 360 video (low complexity, low interactivity) to interactive environments (higher complexity, higher interactivity).
key_concepts: 360 video: capture real environments, limited interactivity, low cost
CoSpaces Edu: drag-and-drop VR environment creation with scripting
A-Frame: web-based VR using HTML-like markup
Social VR: multi-user environments for collaborative learning (Mozilla Hubs, VRChat)
Asset sourcing: 3D models, textures, spatial audio (Poly, Sketchfab)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a VR field trip in CoSpaces Edu: create a 3-room interactive learning environment with informational panels, interactive objects, and a quiz checkpoint
setup_requirements: VR headsets for testing or browser preview

review.value: 360 video captures real places with high fidelity but offers no interaction or agency. Interactive VR allows exploration and manipulation but requires more development effort. Choose based on whether the learning objective requires observation or action.
Drag-and-drop 3D scene builder with CoBlocks (visual programming) for scripting interactions; runs on headsets, tablets, and browsers; designed specifically for K-12 and higher education

class_schedule.topic: Accessibility, Equity, and Inclusion in Immersive Learning
learning_objectives: Identify accessibility barriers in VR/AR learning experiences
Design inclusive immersive experiences that accommodate diverse learners
Evaluate equity implications of VR deployment in educational settings
readings: XR Access Initiative - Accessibility Resources for XR
Mott, M. et al. - Accessible by Design: VR for People with Disabilities

lecture_notes.introduction: VR creates powerful learning experiences but also introduces significant accessibility and equity barriers. Students with visual impairments, mobility limitations, vestibular disorders, or photosensitive epilepsy may be excluded. Hardware costs create equity gaps. Inclusive design must be built in, not bolted on.
key_concepts: Visual accessibility: color contrast in VR, spatial audio alternatives, magnification
Motor accessibility: alternative input methods, seated experiences, one-handed modes
Vestibular considerations: cybersickness disproportionately affects some populations
Cost equity: headset cost, space requirements, technical support needs
Alternative pathways: every VR learning objective must be achievable without VR
lecture_notes.demonstration: Accessibility audit: test a VR educational experience while simulating different disabilities (blindfolded, one-handed, seated-only) and document barriers
setup_requirements: VR headsets with educational content loaded

review.value: The most critical accessibility principle: if a learning objective requires VR, students who cannot use VR are excluded from learning. Every VR experience must have a non-VR alternative that meets the same objectives.

Women experience cybersickness at higher rates than men, possibly due to interpupillary distance mismatches in headsets designed for male averages. This means VR can inadvertently create a gendered experience gap.

learning_objectives: Design research studies to evaluate VR learning effectiveness
Measure presence, engagement, and learning outcomes in VR
readings: Hamilton, D. et al. - Immersive Virtual Reality as a Pedagogical Tool (meta-analysis)
Presence Questionnaire (Witmer & Singer) and IPQ instruments

lecture_notes.introduction: Evaluating VR learning requires measuring not just whether learning occurred but whether VR contributed uniquely. This means controlling for novelty effects, measuring presence as a mediating variable, and comparing against well-designed non-VR alternatives.
key_concepts: Novelty effect: initial excitement that fades with repeated use
Presence measurement: questionnaires, behavioral indicators, physiological measures
Experimental design: VR vs. equivalent non-VR control with same content and time
Transfer: does VR-learned knowledge transfer to real-world performance?
Long-term retention: does immersive learning persist beyond the immediate post-test?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Research design exercise: design a study to evaluate whether a VR chemistry lab simulation produces better learning outcomes than a physical lab, controlling for confounding variables
setup_requirements: Sample published VR studies for critique

review.value: Many early VR studies show large effects that diminish in replications. Studies must include repeated exposure conditions or delayed post-tests to distinguish novelty from genuine instructional benefit.
The ultimate question: do skills learned in VR transfer to real-world performance? Surgical VR training shows positive transfer. The evidence for conceptual learning transfer is less consistent.

quizzes.topic: Immersive Learning Foundations and VR Design

questions.question: A school district proposes spending $50,000 on VR headsets to create virtual field trips to historical sites. Using the DICER framework, evaluate whether this is a justified use of VR. Under what conditions would you recommend proceeding or not?
questions.answer: Using DICER: Historical sites are not Dangerous or Counterproductive. They are accessible via physical field trips (not Impossible, though cost or distance may make them Expensive or Rare for some districts). VR virtual field trips provide 360 observation but limited interaction — much of the same experience could be achieved with high-quality video, photos, and guided activities at lower cost. Recommendation: proceed only if (1) physical field trips are genuinely inaccessible (rural/low-income district), (2) the VR experience includes interactive elements beyond passive observation, (3) the curriculum integrates VR with pre/post activities, and (4) alternative pathways exist for students who cannot use VR. Otherwise, invest the $50K in actual field trips and high-quality multimedia.

rubric.key: Identifies conditions under which VR is or is not justified

questions.question: Describe three specific design strategies to reduce cybersickness in an educational VR experience for first-time VR users.
questions.answer: (1) Use teleportation-based locomotion instead of smooth/continuous movement — teleportation eliminates the visual-vestibular conflict that causes most cybersickness. (2) Maintain a stable reference frame by including a fixed horizon line or virtual cockpit/room boundaries that remain stationary, giving the vestibular system a stable reference. (3) Limit initial session duration to 10-15 minutes with gradual increases — first-time users are most susceptible and may develop tolerance with short, repeated exposures. Additional: ensure frame rate stays above 90 FPS, avoid rapid camera rotations, and provide a comfortable seating option.

rubric.key: First strategy is specific and evidence-based
Second strategy is specific and evidence-based
Third strategy is specific and evidence-based

quizzes.topic: VR Content Creation, Accessibility, and Evaluation

questions.question: Why must every VR learning objective have a non-VR alternative pathway? Identify two student populations who might be unable to use VR headsets and describe what the alternative experience should include.
questions.answer: Non-VR alternatives are required because VR creates accessibility barriers that would exclude students from learning. Population 1: Students with photosensitive epilepsy — flickering screens and certain visual patterns in VR can trigger seizures. Alternative: desktop-based 3D simulation or video walkthrough with the same interactive decision points, narration, and assessment checkpoints. Population 2: Students with severe motion/vestibular disorders — even well-designed VR may cause disabling nausea. Alternative: tablet-based interactive simulation with 360-degree navigation that the student controls without head tracking. Both alternatives must meet the same learning objectives and be treated as equally valid, not as second-class accommodations.

rubric.key: Explains why alternatives are necessary (not optional accommodations)
Identifies two distinct populations with specific VR barriers
Describes alternatives that meet the same learning objectives

questions.question: A study reports that students who used a VR lab simulation scored 20% higher on a post-test than students in a control group who studied the textbook. Is this strong evidence that VR is effective? What confounding variables should be considered?
questions.answer: This is NOT strong evidence that VR specifically is effective. Confounding variables: (1) Novelty effect — the VR group's higher engagement may reflect excitement about a new technology, not instructional superiority. (2) Time-on-task — the VR simulation likely took longer than reading a textbook chapter, so the VR group had more exposure time. (3) Active vs. passive learning — the VR group engaged in active manipulation while the textbook group passively read; the comparison should be VR vs. an equally interactive non-VR activity (e.g., physical lab or desktop simulation). (4) Instructor enthusiasm — teachers may unconsciously invest more effort in the VR condition (Hawthorne effect). A proper study would compare VR to an equally interactive, equally timed non-VR experience with the same content.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the study as insufficient evidence
Identifies at least two specific confounding variables
Describes what a better comparison would look like

major_assignments.title: Educational VR Experience Design and Prototype
major_assignments.description: Design and prototype an educational VR experience for a specific learning context. The project must include a DICER justification for why VR is necessary, a storyboard, an accessibility plan with non-VR alternative, a functional prototype (CoSpaces Edu, 360 video, or A-Frame), and a formative evaluation with at least 3 users.
objectives: Justify VR use for a specific learning objective using the DICER framework
Design an immersive experience applying VR-specific instructional design principles
Build a functional prototype using educator-accessible tools
Ensure accessibility through alternative pathway design
deliverables: Design document with DICER justification (2,000 words)
VR storyboard (5+ scenes with interaction points)
Functional prototype viewable in VR headset or browser
Accessibility plan including non-VR alternative description

criteria: VR affordances clearly mapped to learning objectives
Non-VR alternatives considered and VR superiority justified
Storyboard demonstrates spatial thinking and interaction design
Generative learning activities embedded in the experience
Functional and viewable in VR or browser
Professional quality appropriate to the tool used
Non-VR alternative meets the same learning objectives
User testing conducted with documented observations
Comprehensive accessibility and alternative pathway plan
Rigorous user testing with documented iteration

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on immersive learning theory and VR design

lab_assignments.description: Weekly labs: VR evaluation, 360 video production, CoSpaces building, accessibility testing

major_assignments.description: VR experience design and prototype project

midterm_examination.description: Covers immersive learning theory, DICER framework, and VR design principles

final_project.description: Extended VR project with iterated prototype and evaluation report

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions with VR equipment are mandatory. Equipment is shared and cannot be taken home. More than 2 missed labs will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day for up to 3 days. VR lab access is time-limited and cannot be extended for late work.
course_policies.collaboration: User testing requires peer participation. All design work must be individually produced. Collaborative VR projects are allowed with prior approval.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs must be original. 3D assets must be properly licensed and attributed. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: VR headsets provided in the lab. Personal computer for design work and CoSpaces/A-Frame development. No personal VR headset required.
